system_examination_technique.approach: Cross-system cases require structured adaptation. For valvular heart disease: use cardiac examination technique (see cardiac.json). For COPD: use respiratory examination technique. For proforma-based cases: follow the universal proforma sequence. The key skill is recognizing which system examination to deploy and completing it systematically.
system_examination_technique.sequence: Identify the dominant system → Deploy system-specific IPPA sequence → Look for cross-system complications → Integrate findings into a syndromic diagnosis

actions: Opening: '[Name], [Age]-year-old [Sex], [Occupation], from [Place], admitted on [Date] with chief complaints of...'
Chief complaints: chronological order with SPECIFIC durations.
HPI opener: 'Patient was apparently asymptomatic [X months/years] back, then developed...'
Past history Big 6: DM, HTN, TB, Bronchial Asthma, CAD, Thyroid disease. For each: duration, treatment, compliance.
Drug history: Tab [Drug name] [Dose] — [Frequency as 1-0-1] — since [Duration].
Personal history: smoking (pack-years), alcohol (g/day × years), CAGE questionnaire for alcohol dependence.
Family history: relevant genetic/familial conditions.
GPE: 'Conscious, oriented to time, place, person' — then PICLE + Clubbing at specific sites.
Vitals block: Pulse (rate/rhythm/volume/character), BP, RR, Temp, SpO2, JVP.
Inspection: scars (sternotomy, thoracotomy, pacemaker), visible apex beat, precordial bulge.
Palpation: apex location and character, parasternal heave, thrills, palpable P2.
Auscultation: systematic 4 areas + special positions. Heart sounds (S1, S2, S3, S4). Murmur with ALL 7 features.
Complete: lung bases, peripheral edema, hepatomegaly, blood pressure.
Inspection: chest shape, symmetry, breathing pattern, accessory muscles, scars.
Palpation: tracheal position, chest expansion, vocal fremitus.
Percussion: all zones bilaterally, compare sides, cardiac/liver dullness.
Auscultation: breath sounds (vesicular/bronchial), added sounds (creps/rhonchi/wheeze), vocal resonance.
GCS: E4V5M6 = 15. Always document.
Cranial nerves: at minimum II, III, V, VII, IX/X, XII.
Motor: tone, power (MRC 0-5, proximal and distal), reflexes (deep + plantar).
Sensory: light touch, pain, vibration (128 Hz tuning fork), proprioception.
Cerebellar: finger-nose, heel-shin, rapid alternating movements, Romberg.
Gait: hemiplegic (circumduction), high-stepping (foot drop), waddling (proximal myopathy), broad-based (cerebellar), Parkinsonian (shuffling).
Cardiovascular: [Valve lesion] with [Etiology] — [Complications: AF/HF/PHT] — [NYHA class]
Respiratory: [Condition] with [Etiology] — [Severity] — [Acute/Chronic] — [Complications]
Hepatology: [CLD/Cirrhosis] secondary to [Etiology] with [Complications] — Child-Pugh [A/B/C]
Neurology (UMN): [Syndrome] — [Side] — [UMN/LMN] — [Localization] — [Etiology]
Neurology (LMN/PNS): [Pattern: mono/polyneuropathy] — [Motor/Sensory/Both] — [Etiology]
Renal: [AKI/CKD] — Stage [1-5] — [Etiology] — [Complications]
Endocrine: [Condition] — [Hyper/Hypo] — [Primary/Secondary] — [Complications]

cross_case_differentials.comparison: Valvular Lesions — Quick Comparison (from Valve Atlas)

values: Left sternal edge, sitting forward, end-expiration
Multiple: de Musset, Quincke, Corrigan, Traube, Duroziez, Hill

presentation_script.opening: This reference covers all four major valvular lesions (MS, MR, AS, AR) as a cross-system comparison tool for rapid revision. For detailed case presentations, see cardiac.json individual cases.
presentation_script.gpe_findings: Each valve lesion has characteristic GPE findings. MS: malar flush, low-volume irregular pulse. MR: no specific GPE. AS: low-volume slow-rising pulse. AR: collapsing pulse, visible arterial pulsations.
presentation_script.system_findings: The key differentiator is the murmur + apex beat combination: MS = MDM + tapping non-displaced apex. MR = PSM + displaced hyperdynamic apex. AS = ESM + sustained non-displaced apex. AR = EDM + displaced thrusting apex.
presentation_script.diagnosis_statement: See cross_case_differentials above for comparison table.
differentials: Austin Flint murmur (AR mimicking MS) — distinguish by presence/absence of OS and peripheral AR signs
Functional murmurs (flow murmurs in anemia, pregnancy, thyrotoxicosis — no structural valve disease)
Graham Steell murmur (functional PR from severe pulmonary HTN — EDM at pulmonary area)
HOCM (systolic murmur that increases with Valsalva — opposite of AS)
tips: MS: Loud S1 + OS + MDM at apex with bell in left lateral = the triad. Tapping non-displaced apex confirms.
MR: PSM at apex radiating to axilla. Displaced apex = LV volume overloaded.
AS: ESM radiating to carotids + slow-rising pulse + sustained apex = the triad.
AR: EDM at left sternal edge + collapsing pulse + wide pulse pressure + displaced apex + multiple peripheral signs.

case_scenario.demographics: Reference tool — covers all ages and presentations of valvular disease
case_scenario.chief_complaint: Common presentations: dyspnea (all), palpitations (MS — AF), syncope/angina/dyspnea triad (AS), hemoptysis (MS)
case_scenario.hpi: Key history: childhood sore throats (RHD for MS/MR), age >65 with calcific valve (degenerative AS), young with bicuspid aortic valve (congenital AS/AR), IE risk factors
case_scenario.past_history: Rheumatic fever history, secondary prophylaxis compliance, prior cardiac surgery, IE prophylaxis

clinical_summary.finding: Malar flush + tapping apex + loud S1 + OS + MDM
clinical_summary.detail: Full MS triad with severity markers
clinical_summary.significance: Severe MS if short A2-OS interval, loud P2, parasternal heave

clinical_summary.finding: Displaced hyperdynamic apex + soft S1 + S3 + PSM to axilla
clinical_summary.significance: Severity: displaced apex, S3, loud PSM, AF

clinical_summary.finding: Sustained apex + slow-rising pulse + ESM to carotids + soft A2
clinical_summary.significance: Severe AS: syncope/angina/dyspnea triad. EF preservation until late

clinical_summary.finding: Displaced thrusting apex + collapsing pulse + wide PP + EDM
clinical_summary.detail: Volume-overloaded LV with multiple peripheral signs
clinical_summary.significance: Chronic AR well-tolerated until LV decompensation. Multiple eponymous signs

examination_technique.sequence: General + Pulse → JVP → Precordium: Inspection → Palpation → Auscultation → Lung bases → Edema

actions: Slow-rising (pulsus parvus et tardus) = AS. Assess at CAROTID for character.
Collapsing/water-hammer = AR. Lift arm, wrap wrist, feel thudding pulse.
Low-volume irregularly irregular = MS with AF.
Bisferiens = combined AS + AR or HOCM.
findings_in_this_case: Pulse character directs the differential before you touch the chest

actions: Location: 5th ICS MCL. Displaced = volume overload (MR, AR, DCM).
Character: Tapping = MS (palpable S1). Heaving/sustained = pressure overload (AS, HTN). Hyperdynamic/thrusting = volume overload (MR, AR).
Not displaced + sustained = AS. Not displaced + tapping = MS.
findings_in_this_case: Apex character + location narrows to 1-2 diagnoses

actions: Use BOTH bell (low-pitched: S3, S4, MDM) and diaphragm (high-pitched: S1, S2, AR, MR).
MS: apex, left lateral, bell — MDM with PSA + loud S1 + OS.
MR: apex, diaphragm — PSM radiating to axilla.
AS: aortic area, diaphragm — ESM radiating to carotids. Check for ejection click (bicuspid).
AR: left sternal edge, sitting forward, end-expiration, diaphragm — EDM. Also check apex for Austin Flint.
findings_in_this_case: Special positions are CRITICAL: left lateral for MS, sitting forward for AR

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: Narrowed aortic valve orifice → LV must generate high pressure to eject blood → prolonged ejection time → aortic pressure rises slowly → pulse upstroke is slow with delayed peak and reduced amplitude.
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: [Stenotic aortic valve] → [Prolonged ejection] → [Slow aortic pressure rise] → [Slow-rising low-volume pulse]
findings_with_mechanisms.grading: Pulsus parvus et tardus (small and late). Absent pulse character abnormality does NOT exclude severe AS in elderly (stiff arteries mask it).

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: In AR, blood regurgitates from aorta back into LV during diastole → rapid fall in diastolic pressure → wide pulse pressure → sharp upstroke (high SV from dilated LV) followed by rapid collapse (diastolic runoff back through incompetent valve).
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: [AR] → [Diastolic regurgitation] → [Wide pulse pressure] → [Rapid upstroke + rapid collapse] → [Water-hammer pulse]
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: Also seen in: hyperdynamic states (thyrotoxicosis, anemia, pregnancy, AV fistula, PDA). In AR, look for other peripheral signs to confirm.

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: During isovolumetric relaxation, LV pressure drops below LA pressure. The stenotic but MOBILE valve snaps open under high LA pressure gradient. A2-OS interval inversely proportional to severity — higher LA pressure opens valve earlier.
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: [LV relaxation] → [LA pressure > LV pressure] → [Mobile stenotic valve snaps open] → [Opening snap]
findings_with_mechanisms.grading: A2-OS <80ms = severe MS. A2-OS >110ms = mild MS. Absent OS = calcified immobile valve (advanced disease, unsuitable for PBMV).

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: All result from wide pulse pressure (high systolic from increased SV, low diastolic from regurgitation): de Musset (head bobbing), Quincke (nail bed pulsation), Corrigan (visible carotid pulsation), Traube (pistol-shot sound over femoral), Duroziez (femoral bruit with distal compression), Hill sign (>20 mmHg difference between popliteal and brachial systolic).
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: [AR] → [Wide pulse pressure] → [Exaggerated arterial pulsation throughout body] → [Multiple eponymous signs]

diagnosis_and_differentials.provisional: Valvular heart disease — specific lesion identified by murmur + pulse + apex combination
supporting_evidence: See cross_case_differentials table for complete comparison

differentials.condition: Austin Flint murmur vs true MS
differentials.why_not: Austin Flint: NO opening snap, NO loud S1, HAS peripheral AR signs, HAS EDM at LSE. True MS: opening snap present, loud S1, no AR signs
HOCM: murmur INCREASES with Valsalva (reduced preload). AS: murmur DECREASES with Valsalva. HOCM: bisferiens pulse, asymmetric septal hypertrophy on echo
Functional: soft (<3/6), systolic, no radiation, no associated signs, normal S1/S2, no click/snap, disappears with position change

investigations.expected: Gold standard for valve assessment. Valve morphology, orifice area (MS: MVA), gradient (AS: mean gradient), regurgitation severity, LV function, chamber dimensions, pulmonary pressures
investigations.why: Definitive diagnosis, severity grading, and surgical timing

investigations.expected: MS: AF, P-mitrale (broad notched P in II), RVH. MR: LVH, AF. AS: LVH with strain pattern. AR: LVH
MS: straight left heart border (LA), double density, splayed carina, upper lobe diversion. AR: boot-shaped heart. AS: post-stenotic aortic dilatation
Invasive hemodynamic assessment when echo-clinical discordance

medical.rationale: AF worsens hemodynamics — rapid rate shortens diastole (MS), increases regurgitation (MR)
medical.specifics: Beta-blockers or digoxin. Target HR <80 at rest. Anticoagulation for AF (warfarin, target INR 2-3)

medical.rationale: Reduce congestion in symptomatic valvular disease
medical.specifics: Furosemide for pulmonary/peripheral congestion. Use cautiously in AS (preload-dependent)

medical.rationale: Prevent recurrent rheumatic fever and valve damage progression
medical.specifics: Benzathine penicillin G 1.2 MU IM every 3-4 weeks. Duration: until age 40 or 10 years post-last episode (whichever is longer)

medical.rationale: Prosthetic valves and prior IE require antibiotic prophylaxis for dental procedures

management.surgical: MS: PBMV (percutaneous balloon mitral valvotomy) if Wilkins score ≤8 and mobile valve; surgical MVR if calcified. MR: repair preferred over replacement. AS: AVR when symptomatic (syncope/angina/dyspnea) or EF <50%; TAVI for inoperable. AR: AVR when EF ≤50% or LVEDD ≥70mm or LVESD ≥50mm.
management.this_patient: Management depends on specific lesion and severity — use echo findings + symptoms for timing of intervention.
management.follow_up: Annual echo for moderate disease, 6-monthly for severe. Monitor for progression and new symptoms. Secondary prophylaxis compliance.

vivas.question: How do you distinguish Austin Flint murmur from true mitral stenosis?
vivas.answer: Austin Flint is a functional MDM caused by regurgitant jet of AR displacing the anterior mitral leaflet → simulates MS. Key differences: True MS has opening snap (present) and loud S1 (present). Austin Flint has NO opening snap, NO loud S1, but DOES have EDM at left sternal edge (the primary AR murmur) and peripheral AR signs (collapsing pulse, wide PP). Apex is displaced and hyperdynamic in AR (volume overloaded), but tapping and NOT displaced in MS.

vivas.question: What is the severity assessment for each valvular lesion?
vivas.answer: MS: valve area (severe <1.0 cm², moderate 1.0-1.5), mean gradient >10 mmHg, short A2-OS interval. MR: jet area, vena contracta >0.7cm, EROA >0.4 cm², regurgitant volume >60 mL. AS: valve area <1.0 cm² (severe), mean gradient >40 mmHg, peak velocity >4 m/s. AR: vena contracta >0.6cm, EROA >0.3 cm², regurgitant volume >60 mL, holodiastolic flow reversal in descending aorta.

presentation_script.opening: Sir, I have examined this [age]-year-old [sex] patient who is a [smoking history], presenting with [chief complaint].
presentation_script.gpe_findings: On general examination: patient is [position — propped up/tripod], [breathing pattern — pursed-lip breathing, accessory muscle use]. [Cyanosis status — central cyanosis present/absent]. [Clubbing — grade]. SpO2 [value]%. RR [rate]/min with I:E ratio [value]. Tar staining of fingers. [Flapping tremor — if CO2 narcosis]. Warm peripheries [CO2 vasodilation].
presentation_script.system_findings: On respiratory examination — Inspection: barrel chest (AP:lateral approximately 1:1), subcostal angle >90 degrees, horizontal ribs. Chest expansion globally reduced <2 cm bilaterally. Percussion: hyperresonant bilaterally with loss of cardiac and liver dullness. Auscultation: globally diminished breath sounds, prolonged expiration (I:E 1:3 to 1:4), scattered rhonchi worse on forced expiration. Hoover sign positive (paradoxical inward movement of lower costal margin during inspiration). Signs of cor pulmonale: JVP elevated with prominent 'a' waves, left parasternal heave, loud P2, pansystolic murmur at LLSE increasing with inspiration (Carvallo positive — TR), pulsatile hepatomegaly 4 cm, bilateral pitting pedal edema.
presentation_script.diagnosis_statement: Severe COPD (GOLD Stage [—]) with cor pulmonale, in a chronic smoker.
differentials: Bronchial asthma (reversible obstruction >200 mL AND >12%, eosinophilic, no fixed airflow limitation)
Bronchiectasis (copious purulent sputum, coarse creps, clubbing more prominent, HRCT shows dilated airways)
Interstitial lung disease (restrictive pattern, fine creps, NOT hyperinflated)
Congestive cardiac failure (bibasal creps, S3, displaced apex — may coexist)
tips: Hoover sign is THE sign to demonstrate — it's the most specific bedside sign of severe hyperinflation.
Always separate COPD signs from cor pulmonale signs in your presentation.
If asked about high-flow O2 danger, give ALL THREE mechanisms (Haldane effect is most important, not just hypoxic drive).
Know the ABG interpretation ladder: compensated → acute-on-chronic → decompensated → NIV → intubation.

case_scenario.chief_complaint: Progressive breathlessness (5 years), worsening over 2 weeks with increased cough and purulent sputum (acute exacerbation)
case_scenario.hpi: Progressive exertional dyspnea over 5 years — initially with strenuous activity, now with minimal exertion and occasional rest dyspnea. Chronic productive cough with whitish sputum for >3 years (most days, >3 months/year). Current exacerbation: 2-week history of increased dyspnea, increased sputum volume and purulence (green), low-grade fever. Bilateral pedal swelling noticed 3 months ago. No hemoptysis, no chest pain, no weight loss.
case_scenario.past_history: No diabetes, hypertension. No TB. Occasional episodes of 'bronchitis' treated with antibiotics.
case_scenario.drug_history: Salbutamol inhaler PRN. No regular bronchodilators. Not on LTOT.
case_scenario.social_history: 30 pack-years smoking (1 pack/day for 30 years). Still smoking. No alcohol. Manual laborer (dust exposure).

clinical_summary.detail: Patient exhales through partially closed lips
clinical_summary.significance: Adaptive mechanism generating auto-PEEP — pushes EPP downstream to large airways with cartilage support, preventing dynamic collapse

clinical_summary.detail: Bluish discoloration of tongue and lips

clinical_summary.significance: Diaphragm at mechanical disadvantage (flat) → poor length-tension → accessory muscles recruited
If present: CO2 narcosis (PaCO2 >50). Warm peripheries = CO2 vasodilation

clinical_summary.detail: AP:lateral approximately 1:1, subcostal angle >90 degrees, horizontal ribs
clinical_summary.significance: Chronic hyperinflation — chest fixed near TLC from air trapping
Chest already near TLC → minimal further expansion possible. Bilateral = COPD (unilateral = effusion/collapse/PTX)

clinical_summary.detail: Bilaterally with loss of cardiac and liver dullness
clinical_summary.significance: Excess trapped air reflects sound. Hyperinflated lung covers heart and liver
Air trapping → reduced airflow. Turbulent flow through narrowed airways → rhonchi

clinical_summary.detail: Paradoxical INWARD movement of lower costal margin during inspiration
clinical_summary.significance: MOST SPECIFIC bedside sign of severe hyperinflation. Flat diaphragm loses zone of apposition → pulls ribs IN instead of pushing OUT
RV failure → systemic venous congestion. 'a' waves = RV struggling against high PA pressure
Chronic hypoxia → HPV → vascular remodeling → fixed PHT → RV pressure overload

clinical_summary.detail: PSM at LLSE increasing with inspiration
clinical_summary.significance: Tricuspid regurgitation from RV dilatation. Carvallo sign confirms right-sided origin

clinical_summary.detail: Liver 4 cm, tender, pulsatile. Bilateral pitting edema
clinical_summary.significance: Congestive hepatomegaly (TR transmits to hepatic veins). Systemic venous congestion

examination_technique.sequence: General (position, breathing, cyanosis) → Hands → Chest shape → Expansion → Percussion → Auscultation → Hoover sign → Cor pulmonale (JVP → Precordium → Abdomen → Edema)

actions: Step 1 — General: from foot of bed — position, pursed-lip breathing, accessory muscles, cyanosis, wasting.
Step 2 — Hands: tar staining, clubbing (uncommon in pure COPD — think bronchiectasis or lung CA), flapping tremor, warm peripheries.
Step 3 — Chest shape: barrel chest (AP:lateral ~1:1), subcostal angle >90, horizontal ribs.
Step 4 — Expansion: hands on lower thorax, thumbs midline — globally reduced <2 cm bilaterally.
Step 5 — Percussion: hyperresonant bilaterally, loss of cardiac and liver dullness.
Step 6 — Auscultation: diminished BS, prolonged expiration (I:E 1:3 to 1:4), rhonchi on forced expiration.
Step 7 — Hoover sign: stand at FOOT of bed, watch lower costal margins — paradoxical inward movement = positive.
Step 8 — JVP: elevated, prominent 'a' waves, 'cv' waves of TR.
Step 9 — Precordium: left parasternal heave (RV), loud P2, TR murmur (Carvallo sign — increases with inspiration).
Step 10 — Abdomen: hepatomegaly (tender, pulsatile in TR), ascites.
Step 11 — Lower limbs: bilateral pitting pedal edema.
findings_in_this_case: Parasternal heave + loud P2 + TR murmur

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: Expiratory flow limitation → air trapping → increased residual volume → increased FRC → chest fixed near TLC at rest. AP diameter increases because ribs become horizontal (normally oblique), subcostal angle widens.

Normal dome-shaped diaphragm has a vertical Zone of Apposition (ZOA) against inner rib cage. Contraction pushes ribs OUTWARD. In severe hyperinflation, diaphragm is FLAT → ZOA lost → contraction pulls ribs INWARD instead. Paradoxical inward movement of lower costal margin during inspiration.
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: [Hyperinflation] → [Flat diaphragm] → [ZOA lost] → [Contraction pulls ribs inward] → [Hoover sign]
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: Most SPECIFIC bedside sign of severe hyperinflation. Not seen in mild COPD or asthma (unless severe acute attack).

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: Exhaling against partially closed lips creates back-pressure (auto-PEEP) → increases pressure inside airways → pushes Equal Pressure Point DOWNSTREAM to larger airways with cartilage → prevents dynamic airway collapse → better gas exchange. Also slows expiration.
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: [Pursed lips] → [Back-pressure (auto-PEEP)] → [EPP pushed downstream] → [Large airways with cartilage] → [No collapse] → [Better air exit]
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: Adaptive mechanism — NEVER discourage it. Same principle used by PEEP/CPAP on ventilators.

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: Chronic alveolar hypoxia (PaO2 <60) → Euler-Liljestrand reflex (hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction — unique to pulmonary circulation, initially reversible) → chronic HPV → vascular remodeling (intimal fibrosis, medial hypertrophy — now irreversible) → fixed pulmonary HTN (worsened by polycythemia increasing viscosity) → RV pressure overload → RV hypertrophy → RV dilatation → RV failure.
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: [Chronic hypoxia] → [HPV] → [Vascular remodeling] → [Fixed PHT] → [RV overload] → [RV failure = Cor pulmonale]
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: In cor pulmonale, LV is usually NORMAL → no pulmonary edema, no S3 from LV, no displaced apex. If basal crackles + S3 + displaced apex → biventricular failure, not pure cor pulmonale.

findings_with_mechanisms.finding: CO2 narcosis risk with high-flow O2
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: THREE mechanisms (not just hypoxic drive): (1) Hypoxic drive loss (~25%): chronic CO2 retention resets central chemoreceptors → breathing driven by peripheral O2 sensing → high O2 removes drive → hypoventilation. (2) Haldane effect (~75% — MOST IMPORTANT): deoxy-Hb carries more CO2 → high O2 saturates Hb → CO2 displaced into plasma → PaCO2 rises even WITHOUT hypoventilation. (3) V/Q mismatch worsening: O2 releases HPV → blood flows to poorly ventilated areas → increased dead space.
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: [High-flow O2] → [Haldane (75%) + Hypoxic drive loss (25%) + HPV release] → [PaCO2 rises] → [CO2 narcosis]
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: Target SpO2 88-92%. Use Venturi mask 24-28%. If PaCO2 rising + pH dropping → start NIV, don't just reduce O2.

diagnosis_and_differentials.provisional: Severe COPD with cor pulmonale, acute infective exacerbation, in a chronic smoker
supporting_evidence: Barrel chest + reduced expansion + hyperresonance + loss of cardiac dullness (hyperinflation)
Diminished breath sounds + prolonged expiration + rhonchi (airflow limitation)
Cor pulmonale: elevated JVP, parasternal heave, loud P2, TR murmur, pulsatile hepatomegaly, pedal edema
Current exacerbation: increased dyspnea, purulent sputum, fever

differentials.why_not: COPD: incomplete reversibility (<200 mL or <12% post-BD), structural destruction, smoking history, >40 age onset. Asthma: full reversibility, eosinophilic, younger onset
Bronchiectasis: copious purulent sputum, coarse crackles (not rhonchi), more prominent clubbing, HRCT shows dilated airways. Can coexist with COPD
ILD: restrictive pattern (FEV1/FVC normal or raised), fine inspiratory crackles, NOT hyperinflated. COPD: obstructive, hyperinflated
LVF: bibasal creps, S3, displaced apex, orthopnea. Cor pulmonale: clear lungs, RV signs. Can coexist (biventricular failure)

investigations.expected: Stable COPD: compensated respiratory acidosis (pH ~7.37, PaCO2 ~52, HCO3 ~30, PaO2 ~62). AECOPD: acute-on-chronic (pH <7.35, rising PaCO2, HCO3 partially compensating)
investigations.why: Guides O2 therapy, NIV decision (pH <7.35 with PaCO2 >45 = NIV indication), and severity assessment

investigations.expected: FEV1/FVC <0.70, FEV1 % predicted determines GOLD stage. GOLD 1: ≥80%, GOLD 2: 50-79%, GOLD 3: 30-49%, GOLD 4: <30%
investigations.why: Confirms obstructive pattern, grades severity, distinguishes from asthma (incomplete reversibility)

investigations.expected: Hyperinflation (>6 anterior ribs visible, flat diaphragm), increased retrosternal air space, reduced vascular markings, bullae
investigations.why: Assess hyperinflation, exclude pneumothorax, consolidation, effusion

investigations.expected: P-pulmonale (peaked P in II >2.5mm), RAD, RVH, low voltage (hyperinflated lung attenuates), multifocal atrial tachycardia (AECOPD)
investigations.why: Detect cor pulmonale electrical changes, arrhythmias

investigations.expected: Polycythemia (Hct >55% — chronic hypoxia → EPO → red cell production). Raised WCC in infective exacerbation
investigations.why: Polycythemia is LTOT eligibility criterion if PaO2 55-60

investigations.expected: RV dilatation, RV hypertrophy, TR, elevated RVSP (PASP), normal LV function in pure cor pulmonale
investigations.why: Confirm pulmonary HTN and RV dysfunction, exclude LV pathology

investigations.expected: H. influenzae, S. pneumoniae, M. catarrhalis most common in AECOPD
investigations.why: Guide antibiotic selection if purulent exacerbation

medical.rationale: Correct hypoxemia without causing CO2 narcosis
medical.specifics: 28% Venturi mask. Target SpO2 88-92%. NOT non-rebreather. Check ABG at 1 hour
Prednisolone 40mg PO x 5 days (REDUCE trial). Short course only
Amoxiclav 625 TDS or Doxycycline. Guided by local resistance patterns

medical.rationale: Ventilatory support when pH 7.25-7.35 with PaCO2 >45 despite medical therapy
medical.specifics: IPAP 12-20, EPAP 4-6 cmH2O. Recheck ABG at 1-2 hours. Reduces intubation by 50%, mortality by 50%

medical.rationale: Maintenance bronchodilation based on GOLD ABE groups
medical.specifics: Group A: PRN SABA. Group B: LABA+LAMA. Group E: LABA+LAMA, add ICS only if eosinophils >300. ICS not routinely recommended (pneumonia risk)

medical.rationale: ONLY treatment proven to reduce mortality in COPD
medical.specifics: >15 hours/day. Criteria: PaO2 <55 (absolute) or 55-60 + polycythemia/cor pulmonale (conditional). Measure in stable state ≥3 weeks post-exacerbation

medical.rationale: Only intervention that slows FEV1 decline
medical.specifics: NRT + Varenicline (most effective) + counseling. Never too late

management.this_patient: Acute: Venturi 28% (target SpO2 88-92%), nebs (salbutamol + ipratropium), pred 40mg x 5 days, antibiotics (purulent sputum), ABG at 1 hour. If pH <7.35 → start NIV. DVT prophylaxis. After stabilization: optimize inhalers (LABA+LAMA), smoking cessation, assess for LTOT eligibility, pulmonary rehab referral.
management.follow_up: Spirometry annually, ABG as needed, echo if cor pulmonale suspected, BODE index for prognosis (score ≥7 → lung transplant referral)

complications.mechanism: Infection/pollution → airway inflammation → worsened airflow obstruction → respiratory failure
complications.management: O2 (Venturi 28%), nebs, steroids x5d, antibiotics if purulent, NIV if pH <7.35

complications.mechanism: Haldane effect (75%) + hypoxic drive loss (25%) + V/Q worsening
complications.presentation: Drowsiness → confusion → coma after high-flow O2
complications.management: Reduce to Venturi 28%, START NIV urgently. If GCS <8 → intubate

complications.mechanism: Bulla rupture (especially on positive pressure ventilation)
complications.presentation: Sudden worsening dyspnea, absent BS one side, tracheal shift

complications.mechanism: Chronic hypoxia → HPV → vascular remodeling → fixed PHT → RV failure
complications.presentation: JVP elevation, parasternal heave, TR, hepatomegaly, edema
complications.management: LTOT (cornerstone), diuretics (cautious), treat underlying COPD. CCBs and pulmonary vasodilators NOT proven in COPD-related PHT

vivas.question: What is the Hoover sign and what does it indicate?
vivas.answer: Paradoxical inward movement of lower costal margin during inspiration. Normal: dome-shaped diaphragm contracts → descends + pushes ribs outward via Zone of Apposition. Severe hyperinflation: diaphragm FLAT → ZOA lost → contraction pulls ribs INWARD. Most specific bedside sign of severe hyperinflation. Elicit by standing at FOOT of bed watching lower costal margins.

vivas.question: Why should you not give high-flow oxygen in COPD? Give ALL three mechanisms.
vivas.answer: Three mechanisms: (1) Hypoxic drive loss (~25%): chronic CO2 retention resets central chemoreceptors → breathing driven by peripheral O2 sensing → high O2 removes drive → hypoventilation. (2) Haldane effect (~75% — MOST IMPORTANT): deoxy-Hb carries more CO2 → high O2 saturates Hb → CO2 displaced from Hb into plasma → PaCO2 rises EVEN WITHOUT hypoventilation. (3) V/Q worsening: O2 releases HPV → blood flows to poorly ventilated areas → increased dead space. Target SpO2 88-92% with Venturi mask 24-28%.

vivas.question: Differentiate pink puffer from blue bloater.
vivas.answer: Pink puffer (emphysema): thin, barrel chest, pursed-lip, minimal cough, maintains PaO2 by hyperventilating (pink), cor pulmonale LATE, low CO2. Blue bloater (chronic bronchitis): overweight, cyanosed (blue), copious cough, tolerates hypoxia/hypercapnia, pedal edema, cor pulmonale EARLY, polycythemia, high CO2. These are EXTREMES — most patients are mixed. Modern approach uses GOLD classification.

vivas.question: What is the BODE index?
vivas.answer: Multidimensional prognostic index, BETTER than FEV1 alone for COPD mortality. B = BMI (<21 = 1 point). O = Obstruction (FEV1 %: ≥65=0, 50-64=1, 36-49=2, ≤35=3). D = Dyspnea (mMRC 0-4). E = Exercise (6MWD: ≥350m=0, 250-349=1, 150-249=2, ≤149=3). Score 0-10. Score 7-10 = ~80% 4-year mortality. Uses: prognosis, lung transplant referral (≥7), response to rehab.

vivas.question: What are the indications for NIV in AECOPD?
vivas.answer: Primary: respiratory acidosis — pH 7.25-7.35 with PaCO2 >45 DESPITE optimal medical therapy for 1 hour. Settings: BiPAP IPAP 12-20, EPAP 4-6. Titrate IPAP to improve tidal volume. Contraindications: GCS <8, facial trauma, vomiting risk, hemodynamic instability, undrained PTX. Failure: pH <7.25 or worsening after 2h optimal NIV → intubate. Evidence: reduces intubation 50%, mortality ~50% (Cochrane).

vivas.question: What is the role of ICS in COPD?
vivas.answer: ICS NOT routinely recommended (unlike asthma). Add if: eosinophils >300 + frequent exacerbations, ACO, ≥2 moderate or ≥1 severe exacerbation despite LABA+LAMA. Risks: pneumonia (30-50% increase, especially fluticasone), oral candidiasis, osteoporosis. Withdraw if: eosinophils <100, no exacerbations, pneumonia occurred. GOLD 2023: eosinophils guide ICS — >300 add, <100 withdraw.

vivas.question: Explain the mechanism of cor pulmonale in COPD.
vivas.answer: Step-by-step: (1) Chronic alveolar hypoxia (PaO2 <60). (2) Euler-Liljestrand reflex — HPV, unique to pulmonary circulation, initially reversible. (3) Chronic HPV → vascular remodeling (intimal fibrosis, medial hypertrophy) — now irreversible. (4) Fixed pulmonary HTN, worsened by polycythemia (viscosity). (5) RV pressure overload → RV hypertrophy → RV dilation → RV failure. Key: LV usually NORMAL → no pulmonary edema. LTOT is cornerstone treatment.

vivas.question: What are the criteria for LTOT and why >15 hours?
vivas.answer: Criteria (stable state, ≥3 weeks post-exacerbation): PaO2 <55 mmHg (absolute), or PaO2 55-60 + polycythemia OR cor pulmonale OR PHT (conditional). Why >15h: MRC trial (1981): 15h/day reduced mortality vs none. NOTT trial (1980): continuous better than nocturnal only. Below 15h, cumulative hypoxic damage outpaces repair — HPV is continuous, so O2 must be near-continuous. More hours = more survival benefit.
